name: B.S. in Financial Technology

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: Eight parsed requirement groups deliver an exemplary interdisciplinary build that integrates finance, computing, and analytics with visible prerequisite linkage. A programming spine (CS 100 -> CS 113 -> CS 114) feeds FIN 320 Financial Data Analytics (Python/R), which with FIN 310 Data-Driven Financial Modeling feeds FIN 410 Data Mining and Machine Learning; the finance core (FIN 315 -> FIN 218 -> FIN 430 Options and Futures, FIN 417 Investments) runs in parallel, and emerging-technology courses (FIN 306 Blockchain, MGMT 416 AI for Business Decisions) cap the sequence. The term-over-term progression is coherent and purposeful.
dimensions.suggestion: Make the analytics ladder explicit on the page (CS 100/113/114 -> FIN 320 -> FIN 310 -> FIN 410) so students see the intended data-science-in-finance pathway rather than inferring it from prerequisites.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is sensible for a rigorous interdisciplinary degree, with a deepest chain of 6 that is justified by the programming-plus-quantitative-finance stack (FIN 410 requires FIN 310 and FIN 320; FIN 320 requires a CS course, MATH 333 or MGMT 216, and FIN 218). Grade conditions are explicit and the only dangling references are GER 200/300 placeholders and MGMT 399.
dimensions.suggestion: Clarify the ambiguous 'MGMT 216 and FIN 218 or FIN 315' precedence on FIN 310 with parentheses so the intended prerequisite logic is unambiguous.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data. Despite an outstanding structure, the catalog page states no measurable objectives for what a fintech graduate should be able to do.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 program learning outcomes (e.g., build and validate data-driven financial models, apply machine learning to financial problems, and evaluate blockchain and AI applications in finance).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The eight-term plan is well balanced, with per-term loads of 12-16 credits summing to a correct 120 (summedTermCredits 120). However, statedTotalCredits is recorded as 15.0 - clearly a data artifact, since a B.S. is not a 15-credit program - which produces a spurious creditReconcileDelta of 105 (120 minus 15). The distribution itself is sound; the reconciliation gap is a metadata defect in the stated total rather than a curricular imbalance, but it is a real data problem a reader or the pipeline would flag.
dimensions.suggestion: Correct statedTotalCredits from 15 to the true total (about 120) so creditReconcileDelta resolves to roughly zero and the plan reconciles.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The page is well structured and legible: eight labeled terms, per-term credit subtotals, and clear course titles spanning finance, computing, and analytics. It falls short of exemplary because the statedTotalCredits value is wrong (15) and several GER items carry null credits, and no program description is captured.
dimensions.suggestion: Fix the statedTotalCredits value and complete the blank GER credit fields, and add a short program description framing the finance-plus-technology focus.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum is exemplary for currency in fintech: FIN 306 Blockchain (cryptocurrency and NFTs), FIN 320 Python/R financial analytics, FIN 310 data-driven modeling, FIN 410 data mining and machine learning, MGMT 416 AI for business decisions, FIN 430 derivatives, and SDET 373 web/mobile app development. Required and elective courses closely track contemporary industry practice with few dated emphases.
dimensions.suggestion: Add financial-regulation/RegTech and cybersecurity-for-finance courses so the strong analytics/blockchain stack is complemented by the compliance and security skills fintech employers require.

descriptionQuality.justification: The sampled descriptions are present and specific: FIN 306 explains blockchain, Bitcoin, and NFTs; FIN 320 describes a two-stage Python/R analytics course; FIN 410 details data mining and machine learning for finance; MGMT 416 covers NLP and chatbots. None read as thin placeholders, though some titles are abbreviated ('Fin Data Analytics').

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated clearly with grade conditions and multi-course requirements (e.g., FIN 410 'MATH 111 or MATH 135, FIN 310 and FIN 320'; FIN 320's three-part foundation). The chief caveat is the ambiguous and/or precedence in FIN 310's 'MGMT 216 and FIN 218 or FIN 315', which should be parenthesized.

missingTopics.rationale: The plan is strong on technology and analytics but has no course on financial regulation, AML/KYC, or RegTech, which is essential for fintech careers and pairs naturally with FIN 306 blockchain.
Programming (CS 113/114) and blockchain (FIN 306) are present, but there is no security course, despite fintech's acute need for secure systems and fraud prevention.
FIN 430 derivatives and FIN 417 investments are present, but no dedicated risk-management/Value-at-Risk course covers portfolio and enterprise risk quantification.
A core fintech domain - payment rails, open banking, and API-based financial services - is not explicitly covered in the plan.

missingTopics.topic: Data engineering / cloud for financial data
missingTopics.rationale: MIS 385 covers databases for managers, but there is no modern data-engineering or cloud course for handling large, streaming financial datasets that FIN 320/410 imply.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative AI and LLMs in finance
emergingTrends.rationale: MGMT 416 already covers AI, NLP, and chatbots; extending it to LLM-based financial analysis and robo-advisory would keep the AI emphasis at the frontier.
FIN 306 covers blockchain and cryptocurrencies; deepening into DeFi protocols and smart-contract development reflects where blockchain finance is heading.
FIN 410 machine learning plus FIN 430 derivatives could anchor an algorithmic-trading elective aligned with quant-finance hiring.
AI-driven regulatory technology is a fast-growing fintech segment that the program's analytics and AI strengths (FIN 410, MGMT 416) are well positioned to support.

emergingTrends.trend: Real-time and cloud-native financial data platforms
emergingTrends.rationale: Building on FIN 320, coverage of streaming market data and cloud-native analytics would match the industry's move to real-time financial data infrastructure.

topSuggestions: Correct the stated total credits: statedTotalCredits is recorded as 15 while the plan correctly sums to 120, producing a spurious 105-credit reconciliation gap (creditReconcileDelta 105).
Publish 3-5 program learning outcomes - none appear in the data.
Add financial-regulation/RegTech and cybersecurity-for-finance courses to complement the strong analytics, ML, and blockchain stack.
Resolve the MGMT 399 dangling reference and complete the null GER credit values.
Extend the AI/ML strength toward generative-AI-in-finance and DeFi/smart contracts to stay ahead of the field.
